checks.a_analysis: The overall layout follows the logical order of the source document. Content is organized into sections with appropriate headings and navigation. While the NCDIT logo is moved from the top of the first page to a position after the table of contents, and the layout is single-column, these are expected differences in an accessible HTML conversion and do not impair the user's ability to find information.

Several tables throughout the document are structurally broken. Specifically, in Section 2, the number of columns defined in the 'thead' elements often does not match the number of 'td' cells in the 'tbody' rows. This causes major alignment issues where data appears in the wrong columns. For example, in the 'Desktop and Workstation Options' table (heading-6), sub-rows for Item 1.07 have fewer cells than the main row, causing the Dell part number for the AMD model to be shifted into the 'Configuration' column. Similarly, in Section 3 configurations (e.g., heading-15), rows representing descriptions have only 2 cells while others have 4, leading to visual corruption of the intended grid layout.
checks.b_evidence_source: PDF Page 6, Item 1.07 shows Dell part number 'AB883074 (AMD model, 1.1b)' aligned under the Dell column.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML table under heading-6, the row containing 'AB883074 (AMD model, 1.1b)' only has 4 cells, causing the text to render in the first column ('Configuration') instead of the Dell column.
checks.b_evidence_location: Desktop and Workstation Options, element index heading-6

checks.a_analysis: The primary meaningful image from the source document, the NCDIT logo on page 1, is present in the HTML as a base64 encoded image with appropriate alt text. No other meaningful images (charts, photos) are present in the source PDF.

There is no visual duplication of genuine body content. While some headings are repeated (e.g., 'Portable/Laptop Options'), this corresponds to the PDF spanning these tables across multiple pages and is not a duplication of the data itself. Heading navigation elements correctly point to distinct content sections.
